coredata.dc:title: A 4e-–2e- cascaded pathway for highly efficient production of H2 and H2O2 from water photo-splitting at normal pressure
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yan
Zhao, Yajie
Sun, Yue
Cao, Jingjing
Wang, Hui
Wang, Xiao
Huang, Hui
Shao, Mingwang
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Overall water solar-splitting is one of the most attractive candidates to break water down into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and hydrogen (H2) in a cheapest and cleanest way (i.e., 2H2O →H2O2+ H2). However, the generated intermediate H2O2 in the 2e− process would poison the catalysts. Hence, it is an ideal pathway to generate H2O2 by 4e−–2e− cascaded process (H2O→O2→H2O2), which is more thermodynamically advantageous and can also avoid the catalyst poisoning by the intermediate product of H2O2. Herein, we report CoP modified by carbon dots (CoP/CDs composite) to realize simultaneous generation of H2 and H2O2 via a 4e−–2e− cascaded pathway, coupling photocatalytic water oxidation (4e−) and oxygen reduction reaction (2e−). The optimal CoP/CDs sample displays a prominent H2 yield of 239 μmol/h/g and H2O2 production rate of 466 μmol/h/g without sacrificial agent at normal pressure. It exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity for water decomposition to produce H2 and H2O2 simultaneously. Moreover, the quantum efficiency of the as-prepared sample is 2.05 % at 535 nm. This work offers a novel approach to achieve high-efficiency production of H2 and H2O2 by photocatalytic water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Water oxidation reaction
Oxygen reduction reaction
4e-–2e- Cascaded pathway
At normal pressure

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program ( 2018YFE0306105 ), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51821002 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51725204 , 21771132 , 51972216 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190041 , BK20190828 ), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province ( 2019B010933001 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology , the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the 111 Project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image and (b) the corresponding elemental mapping images of CoP/CDs (C-K, Co-K and P-K correspond to blue, green and red respectively; scale bar: 2.5 μm). (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of CoP/CDs composite (CDs, 8.3 wt %).
(a). The XRD patterns of CoP and CoP/CDs composite. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the CoP/CDs composite: (b) Co 2p, (c) P 2p and (d) C 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) Cyclic voltammograms (CV) curves of CoP/CDs modified GC electrodes (10 μL of 1 mg/mL catalyst solution and 5 μL of 0.5 wt. % Nafion solution) in N2-saturated 0.1 M Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (BMIMPF6) acetonitrile solution (added 5 mg ferrocene as the external standard) with a scan rate of 10 mV/s (a carbon electrode and an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) electrode were used as the counter electrode and the reference electrode).

(a) H2 and H2O2 evolutions from photocatalytic water splitting with CoP/CDs composite (CDs, 8.3 wt %) as photocatalysts (10 mg catalyst in 20 ml deionized water under visible light). (b) Amounts of H2 and H2O2 evolutions of CoP/CDs composite with different quantities of CDs. (c) The cyclic stability of CoP/CDs composite for photocatalytic water splitting under visible light. (d) The AQE obtained with irradiation of different incident light wavelengths (λ = 420, 450, 535, 580 and 630 nm) as function of absorption spectrum of CoP/CDs.

(a) The i-t curves of CoP/CDs composite (8.3 wt %) (red curve) and CoP (black curve) under illumination. (b) Photocurrent curves of CoP/CDs composite (red curve) and CoP (black curve). (c) The EPR spectra (superoxide radical) of CoP/CDs composite. (d) The EIS curves of CoP/CDs composite (red curve) and CoP (black curve).

(a) Transient photovoltage patterns of CoP/CDs, CoP and CDs. (b) Comparison of the in-situ transient photovoltage with CoP/CDs in different reaction conditions a (wavelength of 355 nm and pulse width of 5 ns). (c), (d) RRDE i–t curves of CoP and CDs under dark and visible light.
The photocatalytic mechanism for overall water splitting over the CoP/CDs photocatalyst via 4e−–2e− cascaded pathway. There are two competitive reactions on CDs.

head.ce:title: A 4e-–2e- cascaded pathway for highly efficient production of H2 and H2O2 from water photo-splitting at normal pressure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CoP/CDs composite can realize the simultaneous generation of H2 and H2O2 via a 4e−–2e− cascaded pathway from water photo-splitting at normal pressure.

Overall water solar-splitting is one of the most attractive candidates to break water down into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and hydrogen (H2) in a cheapest and cleanest way (i.e., 2H2O →H2O2+ H2). However, the generated intermediate H2O2 in the 2e− process would poison the catalysts. Hence, it is an ideal pathway to generate H2O2 by 4e−–2e− cascaded process (H2O→O2→H2O2), which is more thermodynamically advantageous and can also avoid the catalyst poisoning by the intermediate product of H2O2. Herein, we report CoP modified by carbon dots (CoP/CDs composite) to realize simultaneous generation of H2 and H2O2 via a 4e−–2e− cascaded pathway, coupling photocatalytic water oxidation (4e−) and oxygen reduction reaction (2e−). The optimal CoP/CDs sample displays a prominent H2 yield of 239 μmol/h/g and H2O2 production rate of 466 μmol/h/g without sacrificial agent at normal pressure. It exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity for water decomposition to produce H2 and H2O2 simultaneously. Moreover, the quantum efficiency of the as-prepared sample is 2.05 % at 535 nm. This work offers a novel approach to achieve high-efficiency production of H2 and H2O2 by photocatalytic water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) energy produced by photocatalysis is a promising manner to fulfil the global growth in clean energy requirements, as well as the environmental problems. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), a valuable chemical for a variety of industries, was currently produced through energy-wasting anthraquinone process [1–7]. In principle, water decomposition driven by solar energy is one of the most attractive candidates, breaking water down into H2O2 and H2 in a cheapest and cleanest way (i.e., 2H2O → H2O2+ H2). However, during this period, the generated H2O2 will poison the catalyst. Therefore, from the point view of photo-electrochemistry, the synchronous efficient production of H2 and H2O2 is one of the ideal paths to solve energy issues.

Overall water splitting on photocatalysts, in one way, can get the H2 and O2 evolution via a four-electron reaction process (i.e., 2H2O → O2 + 2H2). In the other approach, water can be split into H2 and O2 with a two-electron reaction pathway (i.e., 2H2O → H2 + H2O2→ O2) with the valuable intermediate product of H2O2. Unfortunately, it was demonstrated that the produced H2O2 in this two-electron reaction process was decomposed on the catalyst surface [8–10]. Following that, recent researches suggest simultaneous H2O2 and H2 generation in one photocatalytic system driven by visible light two-electron reaction pathway, while these photocatalysts still suffer from very low apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) and low yield of H2O2 in solution, which hinder the utilization of the simple process of photocatalysis for practical H2 and H2O2 production[11].

The formation of H2O2 from water by two-step 2e− process requires energy of 1.78 eV, while, the oxidation of water (O2 as product) just need energy of 1.23 eV by a 4e− pathway. Moreover, H2O2 as an intermediate product is more likely to poison the catalyst in two-step 2e− process [12–16]. Hence, it is an ideal pathway to generate H2O2 by 4e−–2e− cascaded process (H2O→O2→H2O2), which is more thermodynamically advantageous and also can avoid the catalyst poisoning (See supporting information for details).

Carbon dots (CDs) are defined as carbon nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size, and have become a rising star in carbon-based nanomaterials. CDs have been demonstrated to be a functional component in catalyst design [17–22]. In this work, we report a simultaneous generation of H2 and H2O2 by CoP/CDs composite via a 4e−–2e− cascaded pathway, which involves photocatalytic water oxidation (4e−) and oxygen reduction reaction (2e−). The optimized CoP/CDs catalyst, excited by visible light (>420 nm, 9.56 mW/cm [2]), shows high H2 and H2O2 production rate of 239 μmol/h/g and 466 μmol/h/g, respectively, without sacrificial agent at normal pressure. The yields of H2 and H2O2 are five and ten times that of the previous work [11], respectively.

In this catalyst system, CoP serves as the active site for the generation of O2 from water oxidation by a 4e− process. While, CDs show a dual function of both H2 evolution and H2O2 production (by oxygen reduction reaction; 2e− process). Notably, CDs with unique photo-electrochemical properties, make CoP stable, accelerate the electron/hole separation and enhance the photocatalytic activity of CoP. The present work indicates the overall water solar-splitting by a 4e−–2e− cascaded pathway is a promising strategy, breaking water down into H2O2 and H2 in a cheapest and cleanest way.

CDs solution (1 mg/mL) was prepared by electrolysis according to our previous report [23]. The CoP/CDs composite was then synthesized by a modified hydrothermal method [24]. Typically, 1.6005 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.992 g urea were added to 300 mL ultra-pure water under agitation, then added different mass ratios of CDs (1.7, 5, 8.3, 16.6 and 24.9 wt.%) solutions respectively to the above solution with strong stirring for 30 min. The resulting solution was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 150 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was finished, the formed precursor of CoP/CDs composite was washed several times with ultrapure water and ethanol finally dried at 70 °C in the oven. The obtained composite precursor was further phosphided in a tubular furnace. NaH2PO2 and precursor (The molar ratio for Co to P is 1:10) were placed on both ends of the porcelain boat and NaH2PO2 at the upstream side of the furnace. Subsequently, the precursor was heated at 300 °C for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere. Last, the black product was collected, washed three times with ultrapure water and ethanol and dried at 70 °C in the oven. The pristine CoP sample was fabricated under the same conditions in absence of CDs.

The photocatalytic performance was implemented in a multichannel photocatalytic reaction system (PCX50A Discover, Beijing Perfect Light technology Co. Ltd, China) with a white light-emitting diode (420 nm ≤ λ < 700 nm) used as the visible light source. 10 mg photocatalyst and 20 mL ultrapure water were put into a photoreactor vial (50 mL) with a magneton under atmospheric pressure and constant stirring. H2 was detected by online GC-7900 gas chromatograph (GC) [25]. H2O2 was tested by potassium permanganate titration [26]. The reproducibility and stability tests of the photocatalysts were performed by centrifuging suspension after reaction and reused for repeated tests.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was characterized by a PIXcel3D X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean, Holland Panalytical) with Cu Kα radiation (0.154178 nm). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of sample was acquired by a FEI-quanta 200 microscopy with an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were recorded by a FE i-t ecnai F20 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by a Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was finished using a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatised Al Kα X-ray. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were carried out with He I (21.22 eV) as the monochromatic light source. Electrochemical measurements were performed by a CHI760E workstation. In the three-electrode system, platinum wire, saturated calomel electrode and glass carbon electrode (sample) were used as counter electrode, reference electrode and working electrode respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements were conducted on a frequency range from 1 MHz to 0.01 Hz in ultrapure water. The transient photoresponse curves were carried out with 0.1 M sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) and a 300 W Xe-lamp as electrolyte and light source. The transient photovoltage (TPV) responses were collected by a homemade measurement system. The excitation source was a laser radiation pulse from a third harmonic Nd: YAG laser (Polaris II, New Wave Research, Inc.) with a wavelength of 355 nm and pulse width of 5 ns. The TPV signal was registered by a 5 GHz digital oscilloscope (MDO3104, Tektronix).

The morphology and crystal structure of CoP/CDs composite were explored by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CoP/CDs composite displays the regular flowers shape. Fig. S1 exhibits the different magnified SEM images of CoP and CoP/CDs, showing CDs can regulate the morphology of CoP. In Fig. 1b, elemental mapping images of CoP/CDs composite present that C, Co and P are uniformly distributed on the surfaces of CoP. Fig. 1c and d detail the TEM image and the HRTEM image of CoP/CDs composite. In Fig. 1d, the HRTEM image confirms that lattice spacing of CoP and CDs are determined to be 0.28 nm and 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (011) crystal plane of CoP and the (100) crystal planes of CDs, and the result is consistent with the elemental mapping images [27,28]. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore-size distribution curves of CoP and CoP/CDs composite are presented in Figs. S2 and S3, respectively, indicating the CoP/CDs has higher specific surface area than that of CoP, exposing more active sites.

Fig. 2 a indicates the crystal structure of CoP and CoP/CDs composite by XRD, in which the position of the correlation peak matches well with the standard pattern (JCPDS No. 65-1474) of CoP. The diffraction peaks of CoP at 2θ = 31.6°, 36.3°, 46.2°, 48.1°, 52.3°, 56.8°correspond to the crystal planes are (011), (111), (112), (211), (103) and (301), respectively, confirming the synthesis of CoP/CDs. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of CoP are similar to CoP/CDs composite, which reveals that the introduce of CDs cannot affect the crystal structure of CoP. Besides, the XRD patterns of CoP/CDs composite with different CDs contents (1.7 wt. %, 5 wt. %, 8.3 wt. %, 16.6 wt. % and 24.9 wt. %) are shown in Fig. S4. The XRD peak of CDs is not obvious because of the weak XRD intensity and the small amount of the CDs.

The XPS measurement was employed to analyse the surface chemical state and element composition of CoP/CDs composite. Fig. 2b–d respectively show the high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p, P 2p and C 1s for CoP/CDs composite. In Fig. 2b, two peaks in 781.3 eV and 797.2 eV correspond to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 [29], while the other two satellite peaks are located separately at 786.1 eV and 802.3 eV. The P 2p spectrum in Fig. 2c was fitted into two peaks of 129.5 eV and 134.3 eV, which correspond to phosphorus anions of metal phosphides and P-O, indicating the well connection of CoP and CDs [30]. Besides, the C 1s spectrum centred at 288.3 eV, 285.8 eV and 284.3 eV can be assigned to C=O, C-O and C-C from CDs [28]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of CoP and CDs are shown in Fig. S5. As shown Fig. S5a, two peaks located at 780.6 eV and 796.6 eV correspond to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p for both CoP and CoP/CDs are displayed in Fig. S6 to further clearly compare the change in peak position. Notably, the binding energy of Co 2p in CoP/CDs shows 0.7 eV positive shift than that of CoP, implying a strong interaction between CoP and CDs. Remarkably, the P 2p spectrum of CoP in Fig. S5b displayed only one peak in 134.2 eV, which was assigned to phosphorus anions of metal phosphides. Moreover, the C 1s spectrum of CDs shown in Fig. S5c is similar with these of CDs in CoP/CDs composites [31].

The bandgap of a photocatalyst used for photocatalytic water splitting must meet the electrochemical potential E (H+/H2) and E (O2/H2O). The bandgap of CoP/CDs was measured by UV–vis absorption spectra, Cyclic voltammograms (CV) curves and UPS spectrum. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CoP and CoP/CDs composite (8.3 wt %) are showed in Fig. 3 a. As shown, the CoP shows strong light absorption over a long range of light absorption. Notably, the color of sample turns from gray to black after adding CDs, while CoP/CDs composite also has stronger visible absorption than that of CoP, and both of them have strong absorption in the visible absorption range of 500−800 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CoP/CDs composites with different amounts of CDs are shown in Fig. S7, indicating the visible light absorption of the catalyst is improved by compounding CDs.

Fig. 3b shows the cycle voltammetry (CV) curve of CoP/CDs composite, which is used to calculate conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of CoP/CDs composite (detailed calculation shown in supporting information) as 6.10 eV and 4.28 eV, respectively [32]. According to the formula Ec=Ev-Eg, the corresponding Eg of CoP can be calculated to be 1.82 eV. The ionization potential [equal to the valence band energy (EV)] measured by Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) in Fig. 3c. The valance energy of CoP/CDs was computed to be 6.07 eV by subtracting the width of the He I UPS spectrum from the excitation energy (21.22 eV). In Fig. 3d, the CB level of CoP/CDs is positioned above the reduction level for H+/H2 (0 V vs. RHE), and the VB level is located below the oxidation level for O2/H2O (1.23 V vs. RHE). The bandgap structure of CoP/CDs composite satisfies the requirements of photocatalyic water splitting, generating O2 and H2 from water under visible light.

The photocatalytic overall water splitting performance over as-prepared CoP/CDs was evaluated from the output of H2O2 and H2 under visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) without the aid of applied bias potential or any sacrificial agents at normal pressure. The yields of H2 and H2O2 were detected by gas chromatography and permanganimetric method [10], respectively. Fig. 4 a shows the H2 (black line) and H2O2 (red line) evolutions from water with CoP/CDs composite. The effects of the different ratio of CDs in the composite catalysts on photocatalytic overall water splitting were also explored (Fig. 4b). Clearly, the optimal content of CDs is 8.3 wt. %, and the optimal CoP/CDs possesses the highest photocatalytic property with the H2 and H2O2 evolution rates of 239 μmol/h/g and 466 μmol/h/g, respectively. Because the excess CDs can occupy the active sites of CoP, the photocatalytic activity is decrease with the increasing ratio of CDs. In contrast, H2 and H2O2 evolutions of the physical mixture of CoP and CDs are displayed in Fig. S8. As shown, the H2 and H2O2 productions are only a quarter of that of CoP/CDs composite (CDs, 8.3 wt. %), which further confirms that CoP and CDs is synergized by chemical bond instead of simple physical mixture. Moreover, Table S1 exhibits the comparison of the related catalysts performance with CoP/CDs composite, indicating our work provides an economic and clean route to achieve efficient production of H2 and H2O2 at normal pressure. The stability of the catalyst was further studied by cyclic test shown in Fig. 4c. The CoP/CDs composite can maintain good photocatalytic activity after each 12 h and five cycles of test. In order to further explore the intrinsic stability of the catalyst, the XRD patterns and XPS spectra after the photocatalytic reaction were tested (Fig. S9). There was no obvious change in the above test results, which proves the catalyst possesses good stability. Fig. 4d proves that CoP/CDs has favourable light absorption in the long wavelength range, which matches well with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoP/CDs, manifesting the reaction is redox process driven by light [11].

We carried out the electrochemical tests to investigate the superiority of CDs and catalytic mechanism. Fig. 5 a exhibits the i–t curves of CoP/CDs composite (red curve) and CoP (black curve) under illumination. As shown, with the increase of light, the photocurrent gradually increased and did not decrease significantly for a period of time. In Fig. 5b, the i–t curve can also be used to evaluate photocurrent responses of CoP/CDs composite (red curve) and CoP (black curve) with six light-on and light-off cycles. The light response intensity is closely related to photogenerated electrons and holes. It can be observed that the photocurrent intensity of CoP/CDs composite was significantly enhanced compared with CoP under illumination, which proved that CDs can increase the rate of charge separation. The sharper peak of the CoP/CDs is attributed to the change of the surface state by the CDs. To further verify the H2O2 comes from oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), the EPR experiment was carried out under light and dark (Fig. 5c). The EPR results demonstrate that the •O2 − active species exist under light and no EPR signal was detected in the dark. The EIS curve further explored the interfacial charge transfer of CoP/CDs composite (red curve) and CoP (black curve) shown in Fig. 5d. The arc radius of CoP/CDs composite is smaller than that of CoP, which indicates that CDs can accelerate the separation and transfer of charge, so as to improve the photocatalytic performance.

To further insight into the catalytic mechanism of CoP/CDs composite during the water splitting, the in-situ TPV test was carried out. Fig. 6 a shows the CoP/CDs has a stronger photovoltage, indicating that more charge was excited on the CoP/CDs than that of CoP and CDs, which is better for producing H2 and H2O2. Besides, the in-situ TPV was measured in different conditions shown in Fig. 6b. Obviously, the CoP/CDs achieves the highest photovoltage in Nitrogen-saturated anhydrous acetonitrile as no reaction occurs in this case and the generated hole does not consume. With the addition of water, the photovoltage shows a slightly decrease due to the depletion of holes in the process of water oxidation. Besides, when anhydrous acetonitrile was saturated with oxygen, the photovoltage decreased sharply due to ORR. The attenuation of photovoltage in oxygen-saturated anhydrous acetonitrile is more obvious than that of anhydrous acetonitrile with 5 % water, indicating the rate of ORR is much faster than water oxidation, which further reveals that water oxidation is the rate-determing step of photocatalytic overall water splitting. The transfer electron numbers (n) of CoP and CDs were calculated via the RRDE i–t curves in Fig. 6c and d, where n is 3.6 and 1.7, respectively, confirming a 4e−–2e− cascaded process (detailed analysis shown in supporting information). To further confirm this mechanism, the photocatalytic water splitting property of CoP/CDs was also carried out under saturated nitrogen and oxygen atmosphere, respectively (Fig. S10). Noteworthy, oxygen or air saturated atmosphere is more conducive to the produce H2O2, while the nitrogen saturated atmosphere is more favorable for H2 production, which substantiate that oxygen reduction and H2 production are competitive reactions in this condition. The i–t curve of CDs was also shown in Fig. S11, which indicates that CDs can resist the poisoning of H2O2. In addition, the LSV curves of the HER and ORR for CoP/CDs composites were exhibited in Fig. S12 and Fig. S13 respectively, which verified that the as-prepared catalyst has HER and ORR activity furtherly.

A further kinetic analysis suggests that this 4e−-2e− cascaded pathway has a typical kinetic characteristic of series-parallel reaction (the details analysis shown in supporting information). In this system, the O2 produced by water oxidation has no significant effects on the selectivity of H2 or H2O2 production. Nonetheless, the larger the value of O2 reduction rate constant (k1), a larger value of water oxidation reaction rate, and a smaller H2 evolution reaction rate constant (k2) will be beneficial to the formation of H2O2. Furthermore, the more O2 dissolved in water, low pH value and high light intensity will be favourable to produce H2O2. Namely, the smaller the value of k1, a larger of k2 and the less O2 dissolved in water are conducive to the formation of H2.

Based on the above results, we propose a 4e−–2e− cascaded mechanism for CoP/CDs as photocatalyst in simultaneous generation of H2 and H2O2 coupled photocatalytic water oxidation (4e−) and oxygen reduction reaction (2e−). As shown in Scheme 1 , during the process of photocatalytic water splitting, the CoP produces photo-generated electrons and holes under visible light. However, the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes restricts the photocatalytic reaction. After combing with CDs, CDs can accelerate the electron transfer from the conduction band of CoP to the surface of CDs for H2 evolution and oxygen reduction reaction, which largely inhibits the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, the photo-generated holes remained in the valence band will react with water to form oxygen. And the oxygen generated will further be reduced to H2O2 on the surface of CDs by oxygen reduction reaction. Later, we will further optimize this catalyst by adjusting the structure and surface state of the CDs. Furthermore, CDs can be used to modify other semiconductor catalyst to improve the efficiency of photocatalytic water splitting. The ultimate goal of this 4e--2e- cascaded system is to achieve 1.0–2.0 wt. % H2O2 efficiency by photocatalytic water decomposition.

In this work, we designed a CoP/CDs nanocomposite to achieve the simultaneously generation of H2 and H2O2 via 4e−–2e− cascaded mechanism for visible light water splitting, in which CoP serves as the active site for the generation of O2 from water oxidation by a 4e- process and CDs show a dual function of both H2 evolution and H2O2 production by 2e- process. Markedly, the optimal CoP/CDs composite catalyst displays H2 yield of 239 μmol/h/g and H2O2 production rate of 466 μmol/h/g without sacrificial agent at normal pressure. The significant improvement in photocatalytic performance is attributed to the dual functions of the CDs, which can accelerate the electron transfer from the conduction band of CoP to the surface of CDs for H2 evolution and oxygen reduction reaction, simultaneously inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs and improve catalytic activity efficiently. Above all, our work provides an economic and clean route to achieve efficient production of H2 and H2O2 at normal pressure.
Yan Liu: Experiments, Measurement.
Yajie Zhao: Experiments, Measurement.
Yue Sun: Measurement.
Jingjing Cao: Measurement.
Hui Wang: Measurement.
Xiao Wang: Measurement.
Hui Huang: Experiments, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.
Mingwang Shao: Calculation, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.
Yang Liu: Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.
Zhenhui Kang: Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.

Yan Liu: Data curation, Visualization. Yajie Zhao: Formal analysis. Yue Sun: Investigation. Jingjing Cao: Methodology. Hui Wang: Software. Xiao Wang: Validation. Hui Huang: Project administration, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Mingwang Shao: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Yang Liu: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041, BK20190828), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the 111 Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2020.118875.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
